item.title: Airfoil Geometry Optimization via Machine Learning & CFD
item.description:
Overview

This work was part of a VIP research program centered on airfoil geometry generation, aerodynamic performance evaluation, and iterative design improvement. My role focused heavily on the computational pipeline that turns design variables into usable geometry and downstream analysis.

item.title: Airfoil Geometry Optimization via Machine Learning & CFD
item.description:
Overview

This work was part of a VIP research program centered on airfoil geometry generation, aerodynamic performance evaluation, and iterative design improvement. My role focused heavily on the computational pipeline that turns design variables into usable geometry and downstream analysis.

item.title: Ice Accretion Modeling on Airfoil Surfaces Using LEWICE & OpenFOAM
item.description:
Abstract

This work examined airfoil ice accretion behavior using NASA LEWICE together with OpenFOAM-based flow analysis and ParaView post-processing. The project connected prediction tools, geometry preparation, and visual interpretation to study how icing modifies airfoil performance and flow behavior.

item.title: Ice Accretion Modeling on Airfoil Surfaces Using LEWICE & OpenFOAM
item.description:
Abstract

This work examined airfoil ice accretion behavior using NASA LEWICE together with OpenFOAM-based flow analysis and ParaView post-processing. The project connected prediction tools, geometry preparation, and visual interpretation to study how icing modifies airfoil performance and flow behavior.

item.title: Ice Accretion Research
item.description:
Overview

This project focused on airfoil ice accretion modeling using a workflow built around NASA LEWICE, OpenFOAM, and ParaView. The work involved more than analysis alone: a big part of the contribution was making the software stack usable and dependable for the rest of the team.

item.title: Novel Rocket Nozzle Design
item.description:
Overview

This project explores a three-leaf-clover rocket nozzle outlet geometry as an alternative to conventional axisymmetric exits. The objective is to study whether a nontraditional exit shape can meaningfully influence thrust, shock structure, or flow separation behavior across changing pressure ratios.

item.title: Design Class Competition Winner
item.description:
Overview

This project was a rapid design-to-fabrication challenge completed as part of a class competition. The goal was not just to model a concept, but to produce a working machine within real limits on time, tools, and materials.

item.title: Isentropic Flow Parameter Sensitivity in Variable-Geometry Inlets
item.description:
Abstract

Conducted a parametric sensitivity analysis of isentropic flow parameters — total pressure recovery, Mach number distribution, and mass flow capture — across a family of variable-geometry 2D mixed-compression inlets for Mach 2.0–3.5 flight conditions. Results quantify the trade-off between cowl angle, throat area ratio, and total pressure recovery, providing design charts for inlet sizing in preliminary design phases.

item.title: About
item.description:
I’m David!

I’m a mechanical engineering student with a minor in biology, currently focused on propulsion systems and computational fluid dynamics. My work is an intersection of simulation and hardware — I’m equally comfortable running a multi-region CHT simulation in OpenFOAM and machining a design for testing, using a mix of realistic GD&T and imagining new heights for the designs that I create.

item.title: COMING SOON
item.description:
<!-- --- title: "Isentropic Flow Parameter Sensitivity in Variable-Geometry Inlets" date: 2024-08-01 category: "Propulsion · Research" subtitle: "Parametric sensitivity analysis of isentropic flow parameters across a family of variable-geometry supersonic inlets using MATLAB." tags: ["Inlet Design", "Isentropic Flow", "MATLAB", "Parametric Study", "Supersonic"] thumb_color: "linear-gradient(135deg, #0d1a1f, #1e4a5e 42%, #3a8fa8 78%, #7ecde8)" research_badge: true venue: "Undergraduate Research Program — Summer 2024" slug: "isentropic-flow-parameter-sensitivity" aliases: ["/projects/isentropic-flow-parameter-sensitivity/", "/research/isentropic-flow-parameter-sensitivity-in-variable-geometry-inlets/"] links: - label: "Project Repository" url: "https://github.com/" ---
## Abstract
Conducted a parametric sensitivity analysis of isentropic flow parameters — total pressure recovery, Mach number distribution, and mass flow capture — across a family of variable-geometry 2D mixed-compression inlets for Mach 2.0–3.5 flight conditions. Results quantify the trade-off between cowl angle, throat area ratio, and total pressure recovery, providing design charts for inlet sizing in preliminary design phases.
## Motivation
Mixed-compression supersonic inlets achieve higher total pressure recovery than external-compression designs by terminating the final shock inside the inlet duct. However, they are more sensitive to off-design operation and inlet unstart. This study maps the performance landscape to guide early design decisions.
## Methodology
A MATLAB parametric sweep evaluated 480 inlet configurations across:
- **Ramp angles:** θ₁ = 8–14°, θ₂ = 14–22° - **Throat area ratio:** A_throat/A_capture = 0.60–0.85 - **Freestream Mach:** M₀ = 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, 3.5
Total pressure recovery was computed from sequential oblique shock and normal shock relations. Boundary layer displacement thickness was estimated via the Michel criterion.
## Key Findings
- Maximum total pressure recovery of **η = 0.94** achieved at M₀ = 2.5 with θ₁ = 10°, θ₂ = 18° - Recovery drops sharply below 0.85 for ramp angles exceeding 22° at M₀ ≥ 3.0 due to detached bow shock - Throat area ratio is the dominant variable at M₀ > 3.0; ramp geometry dominates at M₀ < 2.5 - Design charts produced for preliminary inlet sizing across the Mach range
## Output
Produced a set of non-dimensional design charts mapping η vs. θ₁, θ₂, and A_ratio for each Mach number, suitable for use in early propulsion system sizing without full CFD simulation. -->
